title: Unusual critical currents in quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum two-width structures in a magnetic field
authors: V. I. Kuznetsov, O. V. Trofimov
abstract: We measured unusual critical currents as functions of temperature in the zero field and as functions of a magnetic field perpendicular to the substrate surface at a given temperature close to the critical temperature in thin-film long quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum two-width structures consisting of narrow and wide wires with different critical temperatures. It is found that the experimental critical switching current as a function of the field at a given temperature, determined by the appearance of a dc voltage on a short section of the structure, is nonlocal (dependent on electron transport in the area containing the junction line between the narrow and wide wires). When current flows through the narrow and wide wires of the structure, the switching currents, experimental and calculated within the framework of the Ginzburg-Landau theory, differ radically from each other. A nonzero switching current exists in high fields greater than the maximum critical magnetic field in a quasi-one-dimensional superconducting wire. In the aluminum two-width structures studied here, the unusual measured switching current challenges description by known theories.
summary: The paper demonstrates that in superconducting aluminum wires of varying widths, the switching current is determined by nonlocal transport at the junction between narrow and wide sections. This behavior contradicts standard Ginzburg-Landau predictions, especially as the current persists in magnetic fields higher than the expected critical field of the narrow wire. The findings suggest that the wide wires significantly influence the superconducting properties via the proximity effect.
main_problem: Investigating the discrepancy between experimental switching currents and Ginzburg-Landau theory predictions in quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum structures with varying wire widths.
main_result: The study finds that the switching current is nonlocal, depending on electron transport in the junction area, and persists in magnetic fields exceeding the maximum critical field of the narrow wire.
method: Measurement of current-voltage (I-V) curves, switching, and retrapping currents under varying temperature and perpendicular magnetic fields, followed by fitting data to Ginzburg-Landau and proximity effect models.
model_or_system: Thin-film aluminum quasi-1D structures consisting of narrow and wide wires (two-width and three-width geometries) in the dirty limit, acting as hybrid SNS or NS junctions.
key_observables: Switching current (Is), retrapping current (Ir), and current-voltage (I-V) characteristics.
important_parameters: Temperature (near Tc), magnetic field (B), wire widths (wn, ww), coherence length (xi), and the proximity effect exponent (alpha).
main_assumptions: The system operates in the dirty limit; calculations of expected currents often neglect the influence of wide wires to highlight discrepancies.
figures_summary: Sketches of the multi-width structures; I-V curves and switching/retrapping current data vs. magnetic field for various configurations (n1, n4a, n4b, sl5, sl6); and comparison of experimental data with Ginzburg-Landau theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem and structure geometry; experimental setup and materials; results for single-width control structures; detailed analysis of two-width and three-width structures under varying magnetic fields and temperatures; discussion of nonlocal effects and proximity effect modeling; conclusion regarding the lack of a complete theoretical framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Solid lines 1a and 1b are the ﬁts of the measured switching current (squares) to the functions IGL(T) (2) at low T and Ir(T) (4) at high T , respectively. The dotted line is the ﬁt of the measured retrapping current (circles) to the function Ir(T) (4). The inset: a sketch of the structure (not to scale) with the measurement circuit.
